steps.title: Isosceles triangles need two equal angles
steps.content: An isosceles triangle has at least two equal angles. With angle $R = 29^\circ$ and the three angles summing to $180^\circ$, we need either $S = R = 29^\circ$, $T = R = 29^\circ$, or $S = T$. The choices fix $S$, so for each one we can compute $T = 180^\circ - 29^\circ - S$ and check whether any two angles match.

steps.title: Test each choice for two equal angles
steps.content:
• Choice A — '$S = 151^\circ$': then $T = 180 - 29 - 151 = 0^\circ$, which is not a valid triangle. ✗
  • Choice B — '$S = 61^\circ$': then $T = 180 - 29 - 61 = 90^\circ$. No two of $29^\circ, 61^\circ, 90^\circ$ are equal, so not isosceles. ✗
  • Choice C — '$S = 58^\circ$': then $T = 180 - 29 - 58 = 93^\circ$. No two of $29^\circ, 58^\circ, 93^\circ$ are equal, so not isosceles. ✗
  • Choice D — '$S = 29^\circ$': then $T = 180 - 29 - 29 = 122^\circ$, and $R = S = 29^\circ$, so the triangle is isosceles. ✓
